name: Al Killian
description: American jazz trumpet player and occasional bandleader (1916–1950)
abstract: Albert Killian (October 15, 1916 – September 5, 1950) was an American jazz trumpeter and occasional bandleader during the big band era.

works.t: The New Moon: Lover, Come Back to Me

about_quotes.speaker: Harry Edison
about_quotes.evidence: we had four trumpets at that time, we had Al Killian, myself, Buck Clayton and Ed Lewis.
about_quotes.doc_title: Harry Edison — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Terry Clark
about_quotes.evidence: Al Killian was killed... we, the band, were pallbearers for Killian. By this time, Marshal Royal is a member of the band... Me and Marshal Royal were around the corner... So we went to work that night, me and Marshal, sharp, sharper than him.
about_quotes.doc_title: Terry Clark — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Charlie Barnet
about_quotes.evidence: Al Killian was still there part of the time. But at that time I think we were using five trumpets... When Killian was in your band did he have those little boxing gloves dangling from his trumpet? Oh, yes. Oh, yes.

about_quotes.speaker: Bill Coleman
about_quotes.evidence: The musicians I knew were Jimmy Hamilton, Sonny Greer, Harry Carney, Ray Nance, Al Killian, Russel Procope, Quinton Jackson, Johnny Hodges, the Duke himself...

about_quotes.speaker: Pete Candoli
about_quotes.evidence: I remember playing the theater in Harlem and Dizzy was there on the band, Al Killian, me and a good trumpet player, a short guy, Jimmy Pupa from Pittsburgh
about_quotes.doc_title: Pete Candoli interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, February 15, 1999

about_quotes.speaker: Quentin Jackson
about_quotes.evidence: Well, the trumpets was Al Killian, Shelton Hemphill, Francis Williams, Shorty Baker, and Ray Nance.
